CHAPTER 37—COMBUSTIBLE FIBERS
—COMBUSTIBLE FIBERS

-

❖ General Comments
Operations involving combustible fibers are typically associated with salvage, paper milling, recycling, cloth manufacturing, carpet and textile mills and agricultural operations, among others.
The primary hazard associated with these operations is the abundance of materials and their ready ignitability. So-called “rag districts,” where cloth scrap and clippings are collected and separated for reuse in paper manufacturing, have been associated with catastrophic conflagrations as recently as the 1970s. Because of these hazards, occupancies storing or handling more than 3 m3 of loose, or 28 m3 of baled, combustible fibers are classified as Group H-3 (high hazard) by SBC 201 under certain conditions. Similar to combustible dusts, the specific manner in which such materials are used and stored must be evaluated.
❖ Purpose
Chapter 37 establishes the requirements for the storage and handling of combustible fibers, including animal, vegetable and synthetic fibers, whether woven into textiles, baled, packaged or loose.
SECTION 3701 GENERAL
3701.1 Scope. The equipment, processes and operations involving combustible fibers shall comply with this chapter.
❖ Fibers and textiles are an integral part of our daily lives. Almost all fibers are combustible. This characteristic is a problem when the fibers are manufactured, made into fabrics and collected as waste.
3701.2 Applicability. Storage of combustible fibers in any quantity shall comply with this section.
❖ Fibers stored as raw material or finished product must comply with this section.
3701.3 Permits. Permits shall be required as set forth in Section 105.5.
❖ The process of issuing permits gives the Fire official an opportunity to carefully evaluate and regulate hazardous operations. Permit applicants should be required to demonstrate that their operations comply with the code

before the permit is issued. See the commentary to Section 105.5 for a general discussion of operations requiring an operational permit, Section 105.5.8 for a discussion of specific quantitybased operational permits for the materials regulated in this chapter and Section 105.6 for a general discussion of activities requiring a construction permit. The permit process also notifies the fire department of the need for prefire planning for hazardous properties.
SECTION 3702 DEFINITIONS
3702.1 Definitions. The following terms are defined in Chapter 2:
• BALED COTTON.
• BALED COTTON, DENSELY PACKED.
• COMBUSTIBLE FIBERS.
• SEED COTTON.
❖ Definitions of terms can help in the understanding and application of code requirements. This section directs the code user to Chapter 2 for the proper application of the indicated terms used in this chapter. Terms may be defined in Chapter 2 or in another SBC Code as indicated in Section 201.3, or the dictionary meaning may be all that is needed (see also commentaries, Sections 201.1 through 201.4).
SECTION 3703 GENERAL PRECAUTIONS
3703.1 Use of combustible receptacles. Ashes, waste, rubbish or sweepings shall not be placed in wood or other combustible receptacles and shall be removed daily from the structure.
❖ The regular and proper disposal of ashes, waste, rubbish and sweepings is of the utmost importance. Not giving a fire a place of origin is the main objective. The waste containers cannot be combustible. Routine and regular handling and disposal of waste products are integral parts of housekeeping.
3703.2 Vegetation. Grass or weeds shall not be allowed to accumulate at any point on the premises.
❖ Tall grass, dry weeds and bushes around buildings and on and alongside roadways, highways and streets present a definite fire hazard. Controlling or clearing grass, weeds

SBC 801-CC-2024

1415

-

CHAPTER 37—COMBUSTIBLE FIBERS

and bushes reduces this hazard.
3703.3 Clearances. A minimum clearance of 900 mm shall be maintained between automatic sprinklers and the top of piles.
❖ Although a 900 mm clearance is required by this section, other sections of the code and NFPA 13 allow less clearance. Sprinkler clearances are intended to provide room for the fire plume to develop, thus ensuring automatic sprinkler activation and preventing obstruction of the sprinkler spray pattern.This section would be more specific to combustible fiber storage and supersede requirements in NFPA 13. See Sections 107.2.1.
3703.4 Agricultural products. Hay, straw, seed cotton or similar agricultural products shall not be stored adjacent to structures or combustible materials unless a clear horizontal distance equal to the height of a pile is maintained between such storage and structures or combustible materials. Storage shall be limited to stacks of 90 metric tons each. Stacks shall be separated by not less than 6 m of clear space. Quantities of hay, straw, seed cotton and other agricultural products shall not be limited where stored in or near farm structures located outside closely built areas. A permit shall not be required for agricultural storage.
❖ This section has basic size and location limitations for hay, straw, seed cotton or similar products to reduce fire risk and facilitate firefighting operations if a fire should occur. However, farm and other agricultural buildings are exempt from the requirements of this section. Agricultural outbuildings probably represent the single largest class of buildings used for combustible fiber storage; however, despite the hazards associated with the spontaneous combustion of green hay, these occupancies generally pose little threat to life or adjacent property.
3703.5 Dust collection. Where located within a building, equipment or machinery that generates or emits combustible fibers shall be provided with an approved dust-collecting and exhaust system. Such systems shall comply with Chapter 22 of this code and Section 511 of SBC 501.
❖ Dust may create a considerable explosion hazard when displaced from a surface on which it rests, and it may also be ignited by static sparks. For this reason, a dust-collecting hood and exhaust system is required.
3703.6 Portable fire extinguishers. Portable fire extinguishers shall be provided in accordance with

Section 906as required for extra-hazard occupancy protection as indicated in Table 906.3(1).

❖ Section 906 gives the requirements for portable fire extinguishers. Such extinguishers must be selected and installed for an extrahazard occupancy because of the high possibility of ignition of combustible fibers or dust.

3703.7 Sources of ignition. Sources of ignition shall comply with Sections 3703.7.1 and 3703.7.2.

❖ This section introduces the provisions for ignition control. By limiting the sources of ignition in the storage or use area, one can reduce the possibility of a fire.

3703.7.1

Smoking. Smoking shall be

prohibited and “No Smoking” signs provided as

follows:

1. In rooms or areas where materials are stored or dispensed or used in open systems.

2. Within 7.5 m of outdoor storage or open use areas.

3. Facilities or areas within facilities that have been designated as totally “no smoking” shall have “No Smoking” signs placed at all entrances to the facility or area. Designated areas within such facilities where smoking is permitted either permanently or temporarily shall be identified with signs designating that smoking is permitted in these areas only.

Signs required by this section shall be in Arabic and English as a primary language or in symbols allowed by this code and shall comply with Section 310.

❖ The three areas listed in this section, because of their hazard characteristics, have been designated to receive “No Smoking” signs, in accordance with Section 310of the code.

3703.7.2

Open flames. Open flames and

high-temperature devices shall not be used in a

manner that creates a hazardous condition. High-

temperature devices and those devices utilizing an

open flame shall be listed for use with the materials

stored or used.

❖ Processes that use open flames must be installed and safeguarded in accordance with the manufacturer’s specifications. Safety is further enhanced by the requirement for certain devices to be listed for use in proximity to hazardous materials.

CHAPTER 37—COMBUSTIBLE FIBERS

SECTION 3704 LOOSE FIBER STORAGE
3704.1 General. Loose combustible fibers, not in suitable bales or packages and stored outdoors in the open, shall comply with Section 2808. Occupancies involving the indoor storage of loose combustible fibers in amounts exceeding the maximum allowable quantity per control area as set forth in Section 5003.1 shall comply with Sections 3704.2 through 3704.6.
❖ This section identifies Section 2808 as the appropriate standard for the exterior storage of combustible fibers. The material hazards presented by combustible fibers are similar to the wood chips, hogged materials, fines, compost, raw materials and solid biomass feedstock regulated by Section 2808. This section further indicates that the more restrictive indoor storage of combustible fibers regulated by Sections 3704.2 through 3704.6 is for those facilities that have amounts exceeding the maximum allowable quantity (MAQ) per control area as set forth in Section 5003.1. If the hazards are controlled so as to eliminate the application of Table 5003.1.1(1) and SBC 201 Section 307.5, the increased protection levels for a Group H-3 occupancy would not be necessary.
3704.2 Storage of 3 cubic meter or less. Loose combustible fibers in quantities of not more than 3 m3 located in a structure shall be stored in a metal or metal-lined bin equipped with a self-closing cover.
❖ Small amounts of material may be stored inside a building in approved containers. Acceptable receptacles include metal or metal-lined containers with self-closing lids that help prevent the loose fibers from being ignited by outside sources such as cigarettes, candles, friction, sparks, etc. The term “self-closing” means that the lid is equipped with an approved device or is otherwise arranged to operate in a manner that will ensure its closing after having been opened. In many cases, these types of bins are top-opening and need nothing more than gravity to meet this requirement. See the commentary to the definition of “Self-closing” in Chapter 2.
3704.3 Storage of more than 3 cubic meter to 14 cubic meter. Loose combustible fibers in quantities exceeding 3 m3 but not exceeding 14 m3 shall be stored in rooms enclosed with 1-hour fire barriers constructed in accordance with Section 707 of SBC 201 or horizontal assemblies constructed in accordance with Section 711 of SBC 201, or both,

with openings protected by an approved opening protective assembly having a fire protection rating of ¾ hour in accordance with SBC 201.
❖ Moderate quantities of material must be stored in 1-hour fire-resistance-rated rooms. Openings in floors, walls and ceilings must be protected with self-closing or automatic-closing 3/4-hour fire doors or windows in approved frames. Section 707 of SBC 201 deals with the construction of and fire-resistance ratings for fire barriers and with fire barriers used for separation of incidental use areas.
3704.4 Storage of more than 14 cubic meter to 28 cubic meter. Loose combustible fibers in quantities exceeding 14 m3 but not exceeding 28 m3 shall be stored in rooms enclosed with 2-hour fire barriers constructed in accordance with Section 707 of SBC 201 or horizontal assemblies constructed in accordance with Section 711 of SBC 201, or both, with openings protected by an approved opening protective assembly having a fire protection rating of 11/2 hours in accordance with SBC 201.
❖ Large amounts of material require a 2-hour fireresistance-rated enclosure. Openings in floors, walls and ceilings require self-closing or automatic-closing, 11/2-hour fire doors or windows in approved frames. Again, SBC 201 establishes the requirements for the construction of fire barriers and required opening protectives.
3704.5 Storage of more than 28 cubic meter. Loose combustible fibers in quantities exceeding 28 m3 shall be stored in rooms enclosed with 2-hour fire barriers constructed in accordance with Section 707 of SBC 201 or horizontal assemblies constructed in accordance with Section 711 of SBC 201, or both, with openings protected by an approved opening protective assembly having a fire protection rating of 1-1/2 hours in accordance with SBC 201. The storage room shall be protected by an automatic sprinkler system installed in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1.
❖ The enclosure requirements in this section are the same as in Section 3704.4; however, if the storage quantity exceeds 28 m3, the storage room must be sprinklered for extra protection.
3704.6 Detached storage structure. Not more than 70 m3 of loose combustible fibers shall be stored in a detached structure suitably located, with openings protected against entrance of sparks. The

SBC 801-CC-2024

1417

CHAPTER 37—COMBUSTIBLE FIBERS

-

structure shall not be occupied for any other purpose.
❖ Separate, special-purpose structures are required for the storage of extremely large volumes of loose material. Only loose fiber material may be stored in the structure, and openings must restrict the entrance of sparks. These occupancies must be classified in Group H-3, and must be constructed and protected in accordance with SBC 201.
SECTION 3705 BALED STORAGE
3705.1 Bale size and separation. Baled combustible fibers shall be limited to single blocks or piles not more than 700 m3 in volume, not including aisles or clearances. Blocks or piles of baled fiber shall be separated from adjacent storage by aisles not less than 1.5 m wide, or by flash-fire barriers constructed of continuous sheets of noncombustible material extending from the floor to a minimum height of 300 mm above the highest point of the piles and projecting not less than 300 mm beyond the sides of the piles.
❖ Bulk restrictions and aisle requirements for piles or blocks of combustible fibers are established in this section. These restrictions provide access for fire fighting and limit the fuel load of piles and blocks. Aisles or flash-fire barriers must be provided between piles and blocks. Aisles must be a minimum of 1.5 m wide to reduce the likelihood of horizontal fire spread between blocks or piles of stored material; and to provide fire fighters access to fires. In place of aisles, protection in the form of flash-fire barriers constructed of noncombustible materials is considered equivalent and may be used as specified in this section. Depending upon the height and array of the baled storage, the provisions of Chapter 32 may also apply to these materials.
Note that these provisions do not apply to densely packed baled cotton based on its exclusion from the definition of “Combustible fibers” in Section 202. See the commentary to the various cotton definitions in Section 202 for a discussion of the hazards of various cotton processing and storage operations.
3705.2 Special baling conditions. Sisal and other fibers in bales bound with combustible tie ropes, jute and other fibers that swell when wet, shall be stored to allow for expansion in any direction without affecting building walls, ceilings or columns. A minimum clearance of 900 mm shall be required between walls and sides of piles, except that where the storage compartment is not more than 9 m wide, the minimum clearance at side walls

shall be 300 mm, provided that a center aisle not less than 1.5 m wide is maintained.
❖ Allowances or clearances must be provided for the expansion of combustible fibers susceptible to swelling when wet. In addition to the lateral forces that these piles may create if placed against structural elements, their contribution to the building live load should be considered. This section prescribes clearances from building elements. Structural loading concerns should be referred to the fire official or to a qualified structural engineer. As an example of special baling conditions, rolled paper is commonly stored on its side rather than on its end to promote water runoff. Structural damage has resulted from the expansion of wet paper rolls or bales of rags when minimum clearances were not provided.

CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories General Comments
—HIGHER EDUCATION LABORATORIES GENERAL COMMENTS

-

❖ Higher education academic institutions often have both basic as well as highly advanced chemistry, biology, medical, engineering and other laboratories where hazardous materials are utilized in a nonproduction environment. Prior to 2018, this code did not specifically address teaching and research laboratories, and prior to 1993, model building codes did not contain requirements related to “control areas.” Users had to comply with the hazardous materials provisions, including quantity limits, which often could be difficult in these specialized academic laboratory settings. It is also difficult to apply the control area limits in buildings that were not built to those requirements, and where control areas might not exist. Also, where institutions are landlocked in densely populated areas, the buildings often must go upward. However, the maximum allowable quantities (MAQs) of hazardous materials per control area, and detailed in Chapter 50, are extremely restrictive on higher floor levels, making it very difficult for laboratories to conduct research in taller buildings. Further, constructing Group H occupancies on higher floor levels can be very challenging as well.
These higher education laboratories are unique environments from a hazardous materials regulation standpoint. While there is often a need for moderate amounts of chemicals, the chemicals are usually in small containers, which are distributed across laboratories on one floor. As a result, the overall density of chemicals is low, which helps minimize the size of any potential incident. Historical fire data over the last 25 years has shown that the vast majority of laboratory fires do not typically extend beyond the area, or even the room, of origin.
Higher education institutions also typically have faculty and staff with extensive chemical expertise. These “Special Experts” often include fire marshals, industrial hygienists, radiation safety officers, biological safety officers, chemical hygiene officers and environmental health and safety officers, all of whom help ensure the safety of researchers, students and visitors, and protect research and the buildings.

A typical multiple-story university science building will house laboratories, office space, storerooms, classrooms, lecture halls and other assemblies. Since the standard control area MAQs are restrictive on upper floors, often designers are forced to locate classrooms and lecture halls on the upper floors so that they can utilize the higher MAQs allowed on the lower floors. This results in moving large numbers of students through hallways, past laboratories to get to the upper floors. They will also have to exit back down the same routes in the event of an emergency.
In response, numerous government jurisdictions have adopted fire and building code amendments and local agreements (grandfathering buildings under the 1997 Uniform Fire Code, for example) to allow different standards for educational laboratories, particularly on higher floor levels. However, this has resulted in varying codes and standards across the country, making it challenging for designers, the academic industry, and fire and building and fire officials. In an effort to develop a standardized model code approach to this problem, a broad group of stakeholders convened, ultimately producing this chapter to address these unique and challenging issues while keeping safety of the occupants a priority.
❖ Purpose
This chapter provides a design option for higher education facilities that need to exceed the standard control area limitations found in Chapter 50. The chapter allows for moderate increases in hazardous materials storage and use but imposes additional construction and operational controls in exchange for the increased allowances. Note that Section 428 of SBC 201 also contains the provisions on laboratory suites, as these are constructionrelated issues.
It is important to note that this chapter does not automatically apply to all new or existing higher education facilities. Only facilities that want to use the increased allowances and can meet the additional requirements would need to comply with this chapter. Fire and building and fire officials would only apply this chapter

SBC 801-CC-2024

1421

-

CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories General Comments

where the higher education facility intentionally chooses to use this option for storage and use of hazardous materials. Essentially, this chapter is an alternative approach to the general hazardous materials requirements focused on a specific type of laboratory.
If a facility does not utilize Chapter 38 for constructing or operating a laboratory, then the laboratories would need to comply with the general building and fire code hazardous materials control area provisions (SBC 801 Chapters 50 through 67, SBC 201 Section 414, and so on) or construct and operate the laboratory as a Group H Occupancy.
SECTION 3801 GENERAL
3801.1 Scope. Higher education laboratories complying with the requirements of this chapter shall be permitted to exceed the maximum allowable quantities of hazardous materials in control areas set forth in Chapter 50 without requiring classification as a Group H occupancy. Except as specified in this chapter, such laboratories shall comply with all applicable provisions of this code and SBC 201.
❖ This chapter provides a design option for higher education facilities that need to exceed the standard control area limitations found in Chapter 50. The chapter allows for moderate increases in hazardous materials storage and use but imposes additional construction and operational controls in exchange for the increased allowances. It is very important to note that this chapter does not automatically apply to any new or existing higher education facilities. Only facilities that want to use the increased allowances and can meet the additional requirements would need to comply with this chapter. Note that the occupancy of these facilities is also regulated by SBC 201 and the provisions that allow increases beyond the control area concept using laboratory suites for new construction are contained in SBC 201 Section 428.
3801.2 Application. The provisions of this chapter shall be applied as exceptions or additions to applicable requirements of this code. Unless specifically modified by this chapter, the storage, use and handling of hazardous materials shall comply with the provisions in Chapters 50 through 67 and SBC 201 for quantities not exceeding the maximum allowable quantity.
❖ Higher education facilities still must comply with all other requirements of Chapters 50 through 67 and SBC 201 except as modified by this chapter. Essentially, this chapter provides

more flexibility with amounts of materials; however, all other provisions in the hazardous materials chapters where the MAQs are not exceeded must be addressed, such as requirements for incompatible materials, signage, release of materials and a variety of other operational requirements.

SECTION 3802 DEFINITIONS

3802.1 Definitions. The following terms are defined in Chapter 2:

CHEMICAL FUME HOOD.

GLOVE BOX.

HIGHER EDUCATION LABORATORY.

LABORATORY SUITE.

SPECIAL EXPERT.

❖ Definitions of terms can help in the understanding and application of the code requirements. This section directs the code user to Chapter 2 for the proper application of the indicated terms used in this chapter. Terms may be defined in Chapter 2 or in another Saudi Codes as indicated in Section 201.3, or the dictionary meaning may be all that is needed (see also commentaries, Sections 201 through 201.4).

SECTION 3803 GENERAL SAFETY PROVISIONS

3803.1 Scope. Laboratories and laboratory suites applying the requirements of this chapter shall be in accordance with the general safety provisions in Sections 3803.1.1 through 3803.2.2.

❖ This section applies to all facilities using the allowances found in Chapter 38. These general requirements apply to any facility using any of the sections in Chapter 38, including newly constructed laboratory suites in accordance with Section 3804 (in new or existing buildings), existing laboratories in nonsprinklered buildings in accordance with Section 3805 or existing sprinklered laboratories in accordance with Section 3806.

Commentary Figure 3803.1 provides a comparison of MAQ possible under Chapter 38 versus traditional control areas as required in SBC 201 Tables 307.1(1) and 307.1(2) and in Section 5003.1.

3803.1.1

Chemical safety reviews.

Operating and emergency procedures planning and

documentation shall be provided in accordance with

Sections 5001.3.3.11 through 5001.3.3.17. Such

documentation shall be prepared by laboratory

SBC 801-CC-2024

1422

-

CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories General Comments

safety personnel or special experts, and shall be made available in the workplace for reference and review by employees. Copies of such documentation shall be made available to the Fire official for review upon request.

❖ This section requires additional safety preplanning for all laboratories or laboratory suites utilizing this chapter, which is consistent with laboratory safeguards found in NFPA 45. Enhanced safety requirements found in this section include the following chemical safety reviews, as identified in Sections 5001.3.3.11 through 5001.3.3.17: process hazard analysis, pre-start-up safety review, operating and emergency procedures, management of change, accident procedures, consequence analysis, and safety audits. The requirement that such documentation be submitted to the Fire official upon request ensures that the facility monitors and analyzes the use of hazardous materials in laboratories regulated under this chapter.

3803.1.2

Chemical handling. Receiving,

transporting on site, unpacking and dispensing of

hazardous materials shall be carried out by persons

trained in proper handling of such materials and

shall be performed in accordance with Chapters 50

through 67, as applicable.

❖ Because higher education facilities are often in mixed-use buildings and chemical transportation routes typically cross through other spaces or areas, persons transporting the hazardous materials must receive training in proper handling.

3803.1.3

Warning signage. Warning signs

shall be provided in accordance with Section

5003.5.

❖ This section refers the user to the warning signage provisions of Section 5003.5. As this chapter still essentially requires compliance with Section 5003 for quantities not exceeding the MAQs, much of Section 5003 will apply to these laboratories. This will include other provisions such as sources of ignition, including the prohibition of smoking.

3803.1.4

Maintenance of equipment,

machinery and processes. Maintenance of

equipment, machinery and processes used with

hazardous materials shall comply with Section

5003.2.6.

❖ This section refers the user to the maintenance provisions found in Section 5003.2.6. Hazards are reduced by having functional equipment and removing and repairing impaired and defective equipment.

3803.1.5

Time-sensitive

materials.

Containers of materials that have the potential to

become hazardous during prolonged storage shall

be dated when first opened, and shall be managed

in accordance with NFPA 45, Section 8.3.4.4.1.

❖ Because certain materials can become increasingly hazardous during prolonged storage, this section mandates additional criteria for these materials and refers the user to detailed requirements found in NFPA 45. The requirements in NFPA 45 focus on identifying which materials are time sensitive, the inspection frequency for such material, defining what inspection procedure should be used and what constitutes passing or failing the inspection.

3803.1.6

Hazardous wastes. Storage,

dispensing, use and handling of hazardous waste

shall comply with this chapter and Chapters 50

through 67, as applicable.

❖ There is sometimes confusion as to whether hazardous wastes are classified as hazardous materials under the fire code. This section makes it clear that facilities utilizing this chapter must treat hazardous waste as a hazardous material and comply with this chapter and Chapters 50 through 67 as applicable based on the hazard class, including complying with the maximum allowable quantities established by this chapter and Chapter 50.

3803.1.7

Automatic fire-extinguishing

systems. New laboratories in new or existing

buildings that increase maximum allowable

quantities of hazardous materials based on the

requirements in this chapter shall be equipped

throughout with an approved automatic sprinkler

system in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1.

❖ In order to use the provisions of Chapter 38 in any new laboratory (whether in new or existing buildings), the entire building shall be equipped with an approved automatic sprinkler system. This is a significant increase in protection from typical B-occupancy laboratories, which can be built under Chapter 50 without sprinklers. Chapter 38 does have limited provisions to allow for existing laboratories in existing buildings to utilize some allowances. The chapter does not provide clarification on what constitutes “new laboratories” in existing buildings, or at what point existing laboratories undergoing modification or tenant improvement would become “new” and require fully sprinklered buildings. The Fire official would make the decision for what constitutes a “new” laboratory.

SBC 801-CC-2024

1423

-

CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories General Comments

3803.2 Hazardous materials storage and use. Hazardous materials storage, handling and use in laboratories and laboratory suites complying with Chapter 38 shall be in accordance with this chapter and Chapters 50 through 67.

❖ Higher education laboratories still need to comply with all other requirements of Chapters 50 through 67 and SBC 201, except as modified by this chapter. The requirements are related to those that apply where the MAQs have not been exceeded.

3803.2.1

Container size. The maximum

container size for all hazardous materials shall be 20 L for liquids, 22.5 kg for solids, 2.85 m3 for

health-hazard gases per Table 5003.1.1(2) and 14 m3 for all other gases in accordance with Table

5003.1.1(1).

Exception: Hazardous waste collection containers, for other than Class I flammable liquids and Class II combustible liquids, are permitted to exceed 20 L where approved.

❖ One of the primary controls in Chapter 38 is to limit maximum individual container sizes. This helps minimize the maximum hazard from an incident involving a container or containers. These requirements are based largely on NFPA 45 limits. It is important to note that these maximum container sizes apply in addition to MAQ limits. They are not an exception to MAQs. For example, if the MAQ for a highly toxic gas is 1 m2, then a 10 m2 cylinder would not be allowed—the maximum quantity and container size are both limited by the MAQ.

3803.2.2

Density. Quantities of Class I

flammable liquids in storage and use shall not exceed 30 L per 10 m2 of floor area. Densities shall be reduced by 25 percent on the 4th through 6th

floors of the building, and by 50 percent above the 6th floor. Regardless of the density, the maximum

allowable quantity per control area or laboratory

suite in accordance with this chapter, shall not be

exceeded.

Exception: Designated hazardous waste collection areas or rooms within a laboratory suite or control area are not limited, but such materials shall not exceed the maximum allowable quantity per laboratory suite or control area.

❖ One of the primary methods to control fire hazards in Chapter 38 is to limit maximum quantity of flammable liquids in any one area of a laboratory. This is accomplished by restricting the maximum density of flammable liquid, which is the same method utilized in NFPA 45. These density limitations apply in

addition to MAQ and maximum container size limits. The density limitations do not provide an exception to either of those other requirements.

SECTION 3804 LABORATORY SUITE CONSTRUCTION

3804.1 General. Where laboratory suites are provided, they shall be constructed in accordance with this chapter and Section 428 of SBC 201.

❖ This section provides the concept of laboratory suites that can be used as an alternative to the general control area concept. The concept is similar to those used for control areas. SBC 201 Section 428 also contains these detailed requirements for laboratory suites, since technically SBC 201 addresses building occupancy and construction classification.

Laboratory suites provide a much higher level of fire and hazardous materials protection than the traditional control area design described in Chapter 50. Besides the additional requirements found in Section 3803, laboratory suites are required to have:

• Rated fire barriers for compartmentation of laboratory suites within buildings.

• Standby or emergency power for safetyrelated equipment and enhanced automatic sprinkler protection in laboratories above the sixth story or in a story below grade plane.

• Automatic sprinkler design and density exceeding that which would be required by NFPA 45.

3804.1.1

Laboratory suites. The number of

laboratory suites and percentage of maximum

allowable quantities of hazardous materials in

laboratory suites shall be in accordance with Table

3804.1.1.

❖ Section 3804.1.1 and Table 3804.1.1 identify the basic design criteria and number of laboratory suites allowed per floor in a building. The table then identifies the percentage of MAQ that is allowed in each laboratory suite. The concept of this table is nearly identical to the control area concept.

A laboratory suite may extend horizontally to encompass multiple laboratories, or an entire floor. A suite may also extend vertically to include more than one floor level. If the floor levels include floors with differing MAQ limits, the code does not specify which limit applies. For example, if a laboratory suite extends vertically to include suites on the third and fourth floors, the code does not prescribe which total percentage of MAQ for the entire suite would apply. The fire or building official should

SBC 801-CC-2024

1424

-

CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories General Comments

make the final determination as to whether the third-floor percentage (100 percent), the fourthfloor percentage (75 percent) or an average of the two (87.5 percent) would be applied. The fire or building official may want to consider the total percent of laboratory suite on each floor or other factors in making this decision. A single laboratory suite may encompass an entire building, as well.
Note c in the table allows the vertical fire barriers between laboratory suites on a floor to be 1-hour rated because of the reduced quantities of hazardous materials in each laboratory suite on the floors above the sixth story, and additional safety provisions in Chapter 38 that apply to all laboratories utilizing this chapter. In addition, chemical exhaust ducts routed through the 1-hour-rated barriers would be permitted to be installed without fire dampers, where installed in accordance with Section 3804.1.1.7. SBC 201 Sections 714.1.1 and 717.5.2, Exception 3, and Section 607.5.5 of SBC 501 allow exhaust system ducts to penetrate fire-rated barriers and fire-rated shafts without a fire damper. These provisions carry significant importance and allow multiple laboratory suites per floor of a building supported by a typical HVAC design. The note has no effect on other provisions of the code and does not change the structural fire-resistance requirements of SBC 201 Chapter 6 or the continuity requirements of SBC 201 Chapter 7.
3804.1.1.1 Separation from other nonlaboratory areas. Laboratory suites shall be separated from other portions of the building in accordance with the most restrictive of the following:
1. Fire barriers and horizontal assemblies as required in Table 3804.1.1. Fire barriers shall be constructed in accordance with Section 707 of SBC 201 and horizontal assemblies shall be constructed in accordance with Section 711 of SBC 201.
Exception: Where an individual laboratory suite occupies more than one story, the fire-resistance rating of intermediate floors contained within the laboratory suite shall comply with the requirements of SBC 201.
2. Separations as required in Section 508 of SBC 201.
❖ This section identifies the required separation of laboratory suites from nonlaboratory suites. For example, the separation of a Group B laboratory suite from a Group A-3 lecture hall

must use the more restrictive of the two identified criteria. Primarily, this is an issue where the separation requirements are more restrictive in SBC 201 Table 508.4 than what this section requires. In many cases, if the laboratory suite is separated from other portions of the building (nonlaboratory suites), then Note c allows a 1-hour separation versus 2 hours, whereas SBC 201 Table 508.4 would require 2 hours in the example of Group B and A-3 occupancy.
The portions of a building that are not laboratory suites would follow all other building and fire code requirements. As related to hazardous materials compliance, other portions of the building that are not laboratory suites would follow standard control area requirements or could be a Group H occupancy. A building can contain both laboratory suites and control areas, but the total combined number is limited in Section 3804.1.1.4
3804.1.1.2 Separation from other laboratory suites. Laboratory suites shall be separated from other laboratory suites in accordance with Table 3804.1.1.
❖ This section identifies the required separation of laboratory suites from other laboratory suites. This, of course, depends on where the laboratory suite is located in the building. From the seventh story on, the separation increases from 1 hour to 2 hours. Note that, as discussed in Section 3804.1.1.1, Table 3804.1.1, Note c allows a reduction to 1 hour where it is simply a separation of a laboratory suite from other portions of the building not containing laboratory suites.
3804.1.1.3 Floor assembly fire-resistance. The floor assembly supporting laboratory suites and the construction supporting the floor of laboratory suites shall have a fire-resistance rating of not less than 2 hours.
Exception: The floor assembly of laboratory suites and the construction supporting the floor of laboratory suites are permitted to be 1-hour fireresistance- rated in buildings of Types IIA, IIIA and VA construction, provided that the building is three or fewer stories.
❖ This section requires 2-hour rated floor assemblies, regardless of the rating required for separation walls. Where 2-hour floor assemblies are required, the structure supporting those floors must also be 2-hour rated. The exception provides the only break from the 2-hour requirement. Where a building is Type IIA, IIIA or VA construction and is three

SBC 801-CC-2024

1425

-

CHAPTER 38—HIGHER EDUCATION Laboratories General Comments

or fewer stories, the floor assemblies can be 1hour fire-resistance rated and supported by 1hour construction.
3804.1.1.4 Maximum number. The maximum number of laboratory suites shall be in accordance with Table 3804.1.1. Where a building contains both laboratory suites and control areas, the total number of laboratory suites and control areas within a building shall not exceed the maximum number of laboratory suites in accordance with Table 3804.1.1.
❖ A building can contain both laboratory suites and control areas, but the total combined number is limited to the maximum number of laboratory suites as identified in Table 3804.1.1. For example, on the second story of a building that contains laboratory suites, it would be possible to have four laboratory suites and two control areas.
3804.1.1.5 Means of egress. Means of egress shall be in accordance with Chapter 10.
❖ Chapter 38 does not modify any egress requirements. Users should refer to Chapter 10 for egress requirements based on the occupancy classification, occupant load and other factors prescribed in the code.
3804.1.1.6 Standby or emergency power. Higher education laboratory suites shall be provided with emergency or standby power in accordance with Section 1203.2.14.
❖ Standby or emergency power is required for any laboratory suite located more than six stories above grade plane or any story below grade plane. Section 5004.7 identifies the types of equipment that must be supplied with standby or emergency power. These provisions are implemented to address the fact that it becomes increasingly difficult to respond to fires where access for the fire department is more difficult. Thus, the safety systems need to able to function longer in order to avoid additional failures.
3804.1.1.7 Ventilation. Ventilation shall be in accordance with SBC 501 and Chapter 7 of NFPA 45.
❖ Laboratory ventilation must be in accordance with Chapter 7 of NFPA 45 and SBC 501. NFPA 45 has ventilation criteria specific to laboratories, including chemical fume hoods, local ventilated enclosures, fume arms, special local exhaust devices and other systems for exhausting air from laboratory work areas in which flammable gases, vapors or particulate matter are released. This provides an

additional level of safety in laboratories. SBC 501 Chapters 4, 5 and 6 contain many of the general construction requirements for ventilation, exhaust systems and duct systems. SBC 501 Chapter 5 addresses requirements for hazardous exhaust systems, and Chapter 7 of NFPA 45 further complements those provisions by providing very specific requirements for the type of system or hazardous material used.

3804.1.1.8 Liquid-tight floor. Portions of laboratory suites where hazardous materials are present shall be provided with a liquid-tight floor.

❖ As an additional safety precaution, Chapter 38 requires a liquid-tight floor in all laboratories or laboratory suites utilizing Chapter 38. This does not require drainage control or secondary containment; it only requires that the floor be liquid tight to prevent spills from penetrating the floor and to allow for cleanup without contaminating underlying materials or floor levels. This can be accomplished with epoxy or other resinous floor systems, impermeable floor materials that are monolithic/seamless or have welded seams, and other similar systems. There are many options available for flooring materials and systems designed specifically for laboratories that meet the requirements of this section. This section also inherently requires that any penetrations are sealed to maintain liquid-tight integrity. The requirement for liquid-tight floors is typically only applicable to situations where the maximum allowable quantities of hazardous materials have been exceeded.

3804.1.1.9 Automatic fire-extinguishing systems. Buildings containing laboratory suites shall be equipped throughout with an approved automatic sprinkler system in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1.

❖ This is a significant modification from standard code requirements (which allow for construction of nonsprinklered laboratories in B or F occupancies). New laboratory suites are only allowed in fully sprinklered buildings. If a user wants to construct new laboratory suites with the additional allowances that Chapter 38 provides, it must be in a fully sprinklered building.

in accordance with Table 3804.1.1.

❖ This section clarifies that the maximum percentage of MAQ allowed in a laboratory

SECTION 3805 NONSPRINKLERED LABORATORIES
3805.1 Scope. Storage and use of hazardous materials in existing laboratories located within existing buildings not equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler system in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1 is permitted where such use complies with Section 3803, Chapters 50 through 67, as applicable, and Sections 3805.2 through 3805.4.
❖ This section allows very limited increases in the quantities of hazardous materials that can be used within existing nonsprinklered buildings, provided that laboratory operations comply with the safety provisions prescribed in this chapter. There are a large number of existing nonsprinklered laboratories in use throughout the United States, many of which were built long before the modern I-Codes, and prior to the adoption of the use of control areas. Under older model codes, buildings could have up to 100 percent of MAQ on upper floors; however, with the more modern I-Codes, that was changed to as little as 5 percent on upper floors. This section allows some increases and modifications in certain scenarios if the additional safety controls are provided. This section is not intended to allow for the construction of new laboratory buildings that are not protected throughout utilizing sprinkler systems and the laboratory suite concept.
3805.2 Nonsprinklered laboratories. The maximum allowable quantities of hazardous materials in storage and use in control areas in laboratories located in buildings not equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler system in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1 shall be in accordance with Table 5003.1.1(1), Table 5003.1.1(2) and Table 5003.8.3.2, except as modified by Sections 3805.2.1 and 3805.2.2.
❖ This section clarifies that the control area provisions established in Chapter 50 apply, except as modified by this section. It is important to note that all of the applicable additional safety controls in Section 3803 must be complied with in order to use the allowances in Section 3805. The primary allowance provided by this section is that Section

3803.1.7 is not applicable, since that section applies to new laboratories (whether in new or existing buildings). Section 3805 is strictly limited to existing laboratories in existing buildings that do not have a sprinkler system installed throughout.

3805.2.1

Restricted materials storage.

Where approved by the Fire official, storage of the

following hazardous materials prohibited by Table

5003.1.1(1) in buildings not equipped throughout

with an automatic sprinkler system in accordance

with Section 903.3.1.1 shall be allowed within a

laboratory control area at 25 percent of Table

5003.1.1(1) limits for a building equipped

throughout with an automatic sprinkler system.

1. Class 4 oxidizers.

2. Pyrophorics.

The percentage of the maximum allowable quantity per control area shown in Table 3805.4 shall be applied to 25 percent of Table 5003.1.1(1) limits for Class 4 oxidizers or pyrophoric materials.

Additional quantity increases shall be prohibited, and such materials shall be stored in accordance with all of the following:

1. Containers shall be completely sealed and stored in accordance with the manufacturers’ recommendations.

2. Storage shall be within approved hazardous material storage cabinets in accordance with Section 5003.8.7, or shall be located in an inert atmosphere glove box in accordance with NFPA 45, Section 7.11.

3. The storage cabinet or glove box shall not contain any storage of incompatible materials.

❖ Where approved by a Fire official, this section allows limited storage of Class 4 oxidizers or pyrophorics in nonsprinklered laboratories where all of the prescribed conditions are met. These hazardous materials have caused many university laboratories difficulty regarding code compliance. The amounts needed for experimentation are typically very limited.

3805.3 Restricted materials automatic fire detection. An automatic fire detection system shall be installed in all existing laboratories in nonsprinklered buildings in accordance with this section. Detectors shall be connected to the building’s fire alarm control unit where a fire alarm system is provided. Detector initiation shall activate the occupant notification system in accordance with Section 907.5 where connected to the building’s fire alarm control unit. Activation of the detection system shall sound a local alarm in buildings not equipped with a fire alarm notification system.

❖ Where storage or use of Class 4 oxidizers or pyrophorics are allowed in accordance with Section 3805.2, automatic fire detection is required in accordance with this section. Typically, the code requires a smoke detection system where an automatic fire detection system is required. Section 907.4.3 addresses situations where a smoke alarm cannot be used and approved heat detectors are permitted.

shall initiate an audible and visual signal at a

constantly attended, on-site location.

SECTION 3806 EXISTING SPRINKLERED LABORATORIES
3806.1 Scope. Storage and use of hazardous materials in existing laboratories within buildings equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler system in accordance with Section 903.3.1.1 shall be in accordance with Section 3803 and with Chapters 50 through 67, as applicable, except as modified by this section.
❖ This section allows very limited increases in existing laboratories located within existing fully sprinklered buildings. There are a large number of existing sprinkler-protected laboratories in use throughout the United States, many of which were built long before the modern I-Codes. Under older uniform codes, buildings could have up to 100 percent of MAQ on upper floors; however, with the more modern I-Codes, that was changed to as little as 5 percent on upper floors. The code recognizes that many laboratories may not be able to meet all of the additional controls and separations required for laboratory suites. This section provides an option to allow for minor increases in MAQ, provided that additional safety controls are provided. It is important to note that all applicable requirements in Section 3803 must also be complied with for a facility to utilize this section.
3806.2 Hazardous materials storage and use. Storage and use of hazardous materials within control areas in new and existing laboratories equipped with an automatic sprinkler system shall

be in accordance with this section and Chapters 50 through 67, as applicable.

Exception: Existing laboratories in buildings equipped throughout with an automatic sprinkler system meeting the requirements for laboratory suites are permitted to comply with Section 3804.

❖ This section gives existing fully sprinklered laboratory buildings three choices:

1. They can continue to operate within traditional Chapter 50 control area limits.

2. They can upgrade the facility to comply with Section 3806, which allows for minor increases in percentage of MAQ in the control areas.

3. They can do more significant improvements and construct laboratory suites and take advantage of larger MAQ increases provided in Section 3804.

CHAPTER 39—Processing and Extraction Facilities
—PROCESSING AND EXTRACTION FACILITIES

-

❖ General Comments
This chapter was created due to the use of materials such as flammable gases and flammable liquids to process and extract fats and oils from plants. Those undertaking these processes are typically unfamiliar with the hazardous materials requirements found in Chapters 50 through 67. This chapter is aimed at providing a framework for compliance focused on the specific industry related to processing and extraction. The occupancy classifications will still be based on the maximum allowable quantities (MAQ) within SBC 201. This chapter focuses on the processes.
Although not referenced by this code, NFPA 36, Standard for Solvent Extraction Plants, can be applied to commercial-scale industrial facilities. Small-scale operations are not covered by the scope of this standard. These operations occur in a wide variety of commercial buildings, including mixedoccupancy buildings. Much of the equipment used in small-batch extraction is not listed by a nationally recognized testing laboratory.
❖ Purpose
Generally, this chapter was developed to deal with processes where those undertaking such processes may be unfamiliar with the hazardous material requirements of this code. In addition, the methods used to process and extract oils may be undertaken with equipment not addressed by the hazardous material requirements of Chapter 50 and materialspecific chapters. This chapter clarifies the minimum code requirements for processing and extraction operations within buildings. This chapter provides a framework for review by the Fire official for these facilities and the equipment they contain.
SECTION 3901 GENERAL
3901.1 Scope. Plant processing or extraction facilities shall comply with this chapter and SBC 201. The extraction process includes the act of extraction of the oils and fats by use of a solvent, desolventizing of the raw material, production of the miscella, distillation of the solvent from the miscella and solvent recovery. The use, storage, transfilling and handling of hazardous materials in

these facilities shall comply with this chapter, other applicable provisions of this code and SBC 201.
❖ This section establishes the scope of Chapter 39 with respect to the use of solvents for the processing and extraction of fats and oils from plants. The requirements in Chapters 50, 53, 57, 58 and 61 must also be complied with where applicable. These provisions are intended to provide a roadmap on the use of the code to address a process not traditionally seen as a hazardous process, but where the risks are such that special attention must be made to address the risks.
3901.2 Existing buildings or facilities. Existing buildings or facilities used for the processing of plants shall comply with this chapter. Existing extraction processes where the medium of extraction or solvent is changed shall comply with this chapter.
❖ This section makes it clear that the provisions are applicable to new or existing buildings. The requirements are based on whether the plant processing is occurring within a building. The applicability is not related to when the building was built. The second sentence focuses on assessing the hazard created by the existing extraction process or changes in solvent utilized for the extraction process. A change in solvent would mean that the extraction process must comply with the current requirements. This is consistent with Section 102.2 addressing applicability.
3901.3 Permits. Permits shall be required as set forth in Sections 105.5 and 105.6.
❖ ❖ The process of issuing permits gives the Fire official an opportunity to carefully evaluate and regulate hazardous operations. Permit applicants should be required to demonstrate that their operations comply with the code before the permit is issued. See the commentary to Section 105.5 for a general discussion of operations requiring an operational permit and Section 105.6 for a general discussion of activities requiring a construction permit. The permit process also notifies the fire department of the need for prefire planning for hazardous properties.

CHAPTER 39—Processing and Extraction Facilities

SECTION 3902 DEFINITIONS
3902.1 Definitions. The following terms are defined in Chapter 2:
• DESOLVENTIZING.
• MISCELLA.
❖ Definitions of terms can help in the understanding and applications of the code requirements. This section directs the code user to Chapter 2 for the proper application of the indicated terms used in this chapter. Terms may be defined in Chapter 2 or in another Saudi Codes as indicated in Section 201.3, or the dictionary meaning may be all that is needed (see also commentaries, Sections 201 through 201.4).
SECTION 3903 PROCESSING AND EXTRACTION
3903.1 Construction. Processing shall be located in a building complying with SBC 201.
❖ As many processing and extraction facilities are “cottage industries” or are part of a new start-up industry, guidance is necessary to clarify that compliance with the applicable requirements of SBC 201 are required. This section essentially sends you to SBC 201. The occupancy classification will be F1 or H based on whether the MAQ have been exceeded. If the MAQ are exceeded, it is possible to have a Group H-2 or H-3 occupancy classification.
3903.2 Prohibited occupancies. Extraction processes utilizing flammable gases or flammable cryogenic fluids shall not be located in any building containing a Group A, E, I or R occupancy.
❖ ❖ This section is consistent with others in the I-Codes in prohibiting potentially hazardous operations from occupancies where occupants are at a greater risk due to population density, lack of mobility or familiarity with the building or other factors. There would be much greater potential for loss of life should such operations occur within buildings containing these occupancies. Processing and extraction systems that utilize flammable liquids or gases for the solvent or involve hazardous processes create the same type of safety concern as other hazardous materials. Similar limits are found throughout the code based on level of risk to certain occupancies. For example, Section 5803.1.1 limits the use of flammable gases in Group A, E, I or R occupancies and also offices in Group B.
3903.3 Location. The extraction equipment and extraction processes utilizing hydrocarbon solvents

shall be located in a room or area dedicated to extraction.

❖ Equipment should be set up in a single location and not be relocated or moved as part of the process, as frequent movement of equipment could lead to failure in hoses or connections, causing a release of flammable solvents. The area should be dedicated to processing or extraction activities to reduce the risk of failure impacting a larger area or putting occupants at risk.

3903.4 Post-process purification and winterization. Post-processing and winterization involving the heating or pressurizing of the miscella to other than normal pressure or temperature shall be approved and performed in an appliance listed for such use. Domestic or commercial cooking appliances shall not be used.

❖ The heating or pressurizing of the miscella can release remnants of the solvent as flammable vapors. Domestic and commercial cooking appliances are not listed for use in flammable vapor atmospheres.

3903.4.1

Industrial ovens. The use of

industrial ovens shall comply with Chapter 30.

❖ Requirements for industrial ovens are covered in Chapter 30. This section requires compliance with those provisions. Essentially, a pointer is provided as to where the appropriate requirements are located for such processing.

3903.5 Use of flammable and combustible liquids. The use of flammable and combustible liquids for liquid extraction processes where the liquid is boiled, distilled or evaporated shall be located within a hazardous exhaust fume hood, rated for exhausting flammable vapors. Electrical equipment used within the hazardous exhaust fume hood shall be rated for use in flammable atmospheres. Heating of flammable or combustible liquids over an open flame is prohibited.

Exception: The use of a heating element not rated for flammable atmospheres, where documentation from the manufacture, or approved testing laboratory indicates the element is rated for heating of flammable liquids.

❖ Flammable or combustible liquids heated up to or above their flash points have the potential to develop vapor-air mixtures above the lower flammable limit (LFL). The use of a listed fume hood prevents an accumulation of concentrations in the flammable range. The use of listed electrical equipment along with the

SBC 801-CC-2024

1434

CHAPTER 39—Processing and Extraction Facilities

-

prohibition on the use of open flame addresses process-related sources of ignition.
The exception recognizes the use of nonlisted equipment where evaluated by an approved testing laboratory as safe for use in flammable vapor environments.
3903.6 Liquefied petroleum gas. Liquefied petroleum gases shall not be released to the atmosphere except where released in accordance with Section 7.3 of NFPA 58.
❖ Venting of LP-gas to the atmosphere shall occur only where the requirements of NFPA 58, Section 7.3 are met. The requirements limit the risk potential of introducing flammable vapors to the interior of the building by: limiting the maximum flow; limiting the release to a finite amount between shutoff valves; using bleeder valves; limiting a release to spaces designed in accordance with Chapter 10 of NFPA 58 to address safety concerns; and purging, in accordance with Section 7.3.2, in emergency situations. These requirements of NFPA 58, Section 7.3 limit the amount of flammable vapor being released or limit such releases to spaces designed specifically for that purpose.
SECTION 3904 SYSTEMS AND EQUIPMENT
3904.1 General requirements. Systems and equipment used with the processing and extraction of oils and products from plants shall comply with Sections 3904.2 through 3904.2.2.3 and 5003.2, and other applicable provisions of this code, SBC 201 and SBC 501.
❖ Devices used in processing or extraction are to meet the requirements of Sections 3904.2 through 3904.2.2.3, Section 5003.2, SBC 201 and SBC 501. The provisions found within Section 3904 recognize that many of the systems and equipment are not typically listed and, therefore, specific requirements are necessary to assist in the evaluation and approvals process for the Fire official.
3904.2 Systems and equipment. Systems or equipment used for the extraction of oils from plant material shall comply with Section 3904.2.1 or 3904.2.2.
❖ Systems or equipment for extraction shall be listed or approved for the use. Section 3904.2 provides two paths for compliance. Section 3904.2.1 addresses equipment or systems that are specifically listed for extraction of oils from plant material. Section 3904.2.2 recognizes that this industry is fairly new and there may be the need to use nonlisted equipment. This

section provides requirements to properly approve nonlisted equipment. Section 3904.2.2 and its subsections contain several additional requirements to provide the same robust design obtained through the listing. This includes prescribing the proper qualifications and approvals process, including a site inspection after installation.

3904.2.1

Listings. Systems or equipment

used for the extraction of oils from plant material

shall be listed and labeled in accordance with UL

1389 and installed in accordance with the listing

and the manufacturer’s installation instructions.

❖ UL 1389 was developed to investigate plant extraction equipment that utilizes flammable solvents. The use of listed equipment and systems provides an approval path that does not require a technical report and registered design professional site inspection since the listing takes into account many of the concerns for nonlisted equipment and systems.

3904.2.2

Approvals. Systems or equipment

used for the extraction of oils from plant material

shall be approved for the specific use. The system

shall be reviewed by a registered design

professional. The registered design professional

shall review and consider any information provided

by the system’s designer or manufacturer. A

technical report in accordance with Section

3904.2.2.1 shall be prepared and submitted to the

Fire official for review and approval. The firm or

individual preparing the technical report shall be

approved by the Fire official prior to performing the

analysis.

❖ Where extraction systems and equipment do not have a listing in accordance with Section 3904.2.1, such systems and equipment shall be reviewed by a registered design professional, typically an engineer. A technical report analyzing the equipment is to be submitted to the Fire official for review and approval. The registered design professional shall demonstrate knowledge of the field and equipment by submitting qualifications to the Fire official for approval prior to submitting the technical report. Essentially, it allows the Fire official to sign off on the technical capability of the reviewer.

3904.2.2.1 Technical report. A technical report, reviewed and approved by the Fire official as required by Section 3904.2, is required prior to the equipment being located or installed at the facility. The report shall be prepared by a registered design professional or other professional approved by the Fire official.

❖ This section is simply ensuring that a technical report is prepared and that such preparation is by a registered design professional. This report is required to be submitted to the Fire official before equipment can be installed, in accordance with Section 3904.2.2. This provides a mechanism to review the safety of the installation by a qualified third party.
3904.2.2.2 Report content. The technical report shall contain all of the following:
1. Manufacturer information.
2. Preparer of record of the technical report.
3. Date of review and report revision history.
4. Signature page, including all of the following:
4.1. Author of the report.
4.2. Date of report.
4.3. Date and signature of registered design professional of record performing the design or peer review.
5. Model number of the item evaluated. If the equipment is provided with a serial number, the serial number shall be included for verification at the time of site inspection.
6. Methodology of the design or peer review process used to determine minimum safety requirements. Methodology shall consider the basis of design, and shall include a code analysis and code path to demonstrate whether specific codes or standards are applicable.
7. Equipment description. A list of every component and subassembly, such as fittings, hose, quick disconnects, gauges, site glass, gaskets, valves, pumps, vessels, containers and switches, of the system or equipment, indicating the manufacturer, model number, material and solvent compatibility. Manufacturer’s data sheets shall be provided.
8. A general flow schematic or general process flow diagram of the process. Post-processing or winterization shall be included in this diagram. Primary components of the process equipment shall be identified and match the equipment list required in Item 7. Operating temperatures, pressures and solvent state of matter shall be identified in each primary step or component. A piping and instrumentation diagram (PID or P&ID) shall be provided.

9. 9.Analysis of the vessel(s) if pressurized beyond standard atmospheric pressure. Analysis shall include purchased and fabricated components.
10. Structural analysis for the frame system supporting the equipment.
11. Process safety analysis of the extraction system, from the introduction of raw product to the end of the extraction process.
12. Comprehensive process hazard analysis considering failure modes and points of failure throughout the process. The process hazard analysis shall include a review of emergency procedure information provided by the manufacturer of the equipment or process and not that of the facility, building or room.
13. Review of the assembly instructions, operational and maintenance manuals provided by the manufacturer.
14. List of references used in the analysis.
❖ This section specifies the information to be presented in the technical report. The information to be provided includes many pieces of information related to the equipment. Since the equipment is not listed to a specific standard, the technical report needs to be robust in its requirements to properly evaluate the safety of the system and equipment. The report addresses items such as the manufacturer; the preparer of the technical report; date the report was prepared; signature and information on registered design professional; model number of the unit evaluated; methodology used for the evaluation; a description of equipment; a process flow schematic; analysis of pressurized vessels; structural analysis of equipment support frames; a complete process analysis; a hazard analysis that covers failure modes and points of failure; a review of assembly instructions and a list of the references used in the preparation of the report. This is necessary documentation for often unique designs. This also provides a consistent approach for any similar operations within a jurisdiction.
3904.2.2.3 Site inspection. Prior to operation of the extraction equipment, where required by the Fire official, the engineer of record or approved professional, as approved in Section 3904.2, shall inspect the site of the extraction process once equipment has been installed for compliance with the technical report and the building analysis. The

engineer of record or approved professional shall provide a report of findings and observations of the site inspection to the Fire official prior to the approval of the extraction process. The field inspection report authored by the engineer of record shall include the serial number of the equipment used in the process and shall confirm that the equipment installed is the same model and type of equipment identified in the technical report.

❖ The Fire official can require that the engineer of record or approved professional conduct a site visit to verify installation of the equipment. A report verifying that the equipment was installed per the technical report shall be submitted. This is necessary to ensure that the systems and associated equipment are constructed and in use as approved, which provides a level of accountability.

SECTION 3905 SAFETY SYSTEMS

3905.1 Gas detection. For extraction processes utilizing flammable gases as solvents, a gas detection system complying with Section 916 shall be provided.

❖ Where a flammable gas is used as a solvent, a continuous gas detection system shall be provided in accordance with Section 916. To prevent any accidental or planned release of flammable gases from reaching the lower explosive limit/lower flammability limit (LEL/LFL),the detection shall be set at 25 percent of the LEL/LFL. This criterion is found in Section 916.8. Sections 3905.1.1 through 3905.2 address the specific requirements associated with processing and extraction.

3905.1.1

Operation. Activation of the gas

detection system shall result in all the following:

1. Initiation of distinct audible and visual alarm signals in the extraction room.

2. Deactivation of all heating systems located in the extraction room.

3. Activation of the mechanical ventilation system, where the system is interlocked with gas detection.

4. De-energize all light switches and electrical outlets.

❖ The required local alarm is intended to alert the occupants to an emerging hazardous condition in the vicinity. As a safety precaution, heating systems are required to be deactivated and light switches and electrical outlets to be deenergized. Both are potential ignition sources when flammable vapors are present. In addition to causing alarms to activate, the detection system must also activate the mechanical ventilation system.

3905.1.2

Failure of the gas detection

system. Failure of the gas detection system shall

result in the deactivation of the heating system;

activation of the mechanical ventilation system

where the system is interlocked with the gas

detection system; and initiation of a trouble signal

to sound in an approved location.

❖ as detection systems must be designed to be selfmonitoring and fail safe in that if the gas detection system is not operational the heat must be shut down and the ventilation system must operate. An alarm to the occupants is not necessary, but a trouble signal must be provided to ensure that the situation is rectified.

3905.2 Emergency shutoff. Extraction processes utilizing gaseous hydrocarbon-based solvents shall be provided with emergency shutoff systems in accordance with Section 5803.1.3.

❖ In order to maintain control over the flow of flammable gases under emergency conditions, each supply source, as well as each point of use of flammable gas, must have an approved emergency shutoff valve. These valves must be excess flow control valves to regulate the rate of flow of hazardous materials within the piping system, fail-safe valves or other approved types that will operate automatically. There must also be a manual means of operation to stop the flow.
